steps.action: Weapon dances incorporating swords or similar we with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: Weapon dances incorporating swords or similar we with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: There are various traditions of solo and mock-ba
steps.text: There are various traditions of solo and mock-battle (Pyrrhic) sword dances in Africa, Asia and Europe.

steps.action: hilt-and-point sword dances – where the dancers
steps.text: hilt-and-point sword dances – where the dancers are linked together by their swords in a chain.

steps.action: Jian wu was one of four classical dances that we
steps.text: Jian wu was one of four classical dances that were used in the Chinese and Vietnamese opera.

steps.action: Each of these dances was very meaningful within
steps.text: Each of these dances was very meaningful within the opera performances and they often were used for plot descriptions and characterization.

steps.action: There are quite a few styles within the actual a
steps.text: There are quite a few styles within the actual art of sword dancing.

steps.action: There are also different types of swords and wea
steps.text: There are also different types of swords and weapons used during each dance.

steps.action: A long sword is usually wielded with slower dram
steps.text: A long sword is usually wielded with slower dramatic movements during a solo performance while shorter scimitars are used at a very high pace during a sword dance between two or more people.

steps.action: Each style requires certain movements that requi
steps.text: Each style requires certain movements that require the dancer to be highly agile and athletic.

steps.action: The paika akhada which were previously used to t
steps.text: The paika akhada which were previously used to train Orissan warriors now perform weapon dances in the streets during festivals.

steps.action: Other dances like the mer dandiya are simply the
steps.text: Other dances like the mer dandiya are simply theatrical depictions of battle, while some like the choliya of the Kumaon region were used to ward off evil spirits.

steps.action: Sword dances are still commonly performed for we
steps.text: Sword dances are still commonly performed for weddings and other occasions in the Indian subcontinent today.

steps.action: Female sword dancing was not widespread in West
steps.text: Female sword dancing was not widespread in West Asia.

steps.action: Men in Egypt performed a dance called el ard, a
steps.text: Men in Egypt performed a dance called el ard, a martial dance involving upraised swords, but women were not widely known to use swords as props during their dancing in public.

steps.action: However, paintings and engravings by French arti
steps.text: However, paintings and engravings by French artist Jean-Léon Gérôme (who visited Egypt in the 18th century) show sword dancers balancing sabers on their heads.

steps.action: The Turkish Kılıç Kalkan dance of Bursa is perfo
steps.text: The Turkish Kılıç Kalkan dance of Bursa is performed exclusively by men with a sword and shield, and represents the Ottoman conquest of the city.

steps.action: The performers wear early Ottoman battle dress a
steps.text: The performers wear early Ottoman battle dress and dance to the sound of clashing swords and shields without music.

steps.action: Sword dances are also performed by Albanians, bo
steps.text: Sword dances are also performed by Albanians, both in the Balkans and in Italy.

steps.action: Into the late 1400s Albanian sword dances were i
steps.text: Into the late 1400s Albanian sword dances were imitated by Italian tarantellas.

steps.action: Sword dances performed by the guilds of Smiths a
steps.text: Sword dances performed by the guilds of Smiths and Cutlers in Nuremberg are recorded from 1350.

steps.action: 16th century records of sword dances survive fro
steps.text: 16th century records of sword dances survive from all over Germany.

steps.action: Depictions of dances survive from Zürich (1578)
steps.text: Depictions of dances survive from Zürich (1578) and Nuremberg (1600).

steps.action: In Scotland a dance was recorded as being perfor
steps.text: In Scotland a dance was recorded as being performed in 1285, but this was found in a document from 1440.

steps.action: An important concentration of traditional sword
steps.text: An important concentration of traditional sword dances can be found on the Italian side of the western Alps.
